context.description: The page reflects on altered consciousness, asking whether MRI patterns can be identified across people and why the brain can generate geometric and spatial constructs it later cannot explain. It cites reports on LSD and DMT experiences, including unusual visual geometry and altered spatial perception.
normalization_notes: Source language is German; summary translated to English.

rows.paragraph_text: It is completely unclear. Everyone may have their own ideas about it, but there are no correct rules that can be applied universally, written down academically, and referred to.
Could an MRI pattern be identified in all people who change their awareness?

Given a technology that can roughly image brain activity and is used to watch the brain's Christmas-tree-like lighting on LSD, why not apply it to other states as well? Perhaps there are patterns that appear in the same way for everyone when awareness changes.
Why can our brain invent constructs that it cannot later understand itself?

On certain drugs the brain seems to understand geometry that does not fit our three-dimensional understanding, and ordinary spatial perception can shift so that objects or spaces appear without a back side and in their entirety.
The author argues that these are not isolated cases and points to Erowid.org and user reports on DMT and LSD as evidence of recurring geometric and spatial experiences.

content.free_text: book_page consciousness_and_mind neuroscience_reflection Book page from Bewusstseinsreisen - Version 2 on altered consciousness, MRI patterns, drug-induced visual geometry, and reports about DMT and LSD experiences.
